Сделай рерайт в формальном стиле на английском языке,  чтобы максимально лаконично звучало для носителей языка. Где нужно измени и подправь. Чтобы было правильно для носителей "Translated from Norwegian, the name of this tiara means "fan," and it fully lives up to its name in appearance. This unique jewel is much smaller than traditional diadems and is made of gold, silver, and diamonds. It can be worn in the hair or attached to a necklace. For a long time, it was believed that the tiara was commissioned by Queen Victoria in 1887 and presented as a gift to her granddaughter on her coming of age.

Moreover, in one of the few photographs of Queen Maud wearing the tiara, she can be seen wearing it approximately at the time when the family was in mourning for Queen Victoria in 1901. One might have thought that she wore the tiara in memory of her grandmother. However, it seems that this theory turned out to be incorrect.

Chatter over at the Royal Jewels of the World Message Board has raised new possibilities about its origins.

In reality, this tiara was given to Princess Maud on the occasion of her wedding on July 22, 1896. The gift came from the Rothschild family. 

The Vifte Tiara was among the jewels inherited by Crown Prince Harald and began to be worn by Crown Princess Sonja soon afterwards.

In 2001, King Harald and Queen Sonja gave the Vifte Tiara to Mette-Marit Tjessem for her Wedding to Crown Prince Haakon of Norway in 2001, with the piece worn as a necklace. The necklace was also worn again for a State Banquet in 2006.

The only time that Crown Princess Mette-Marit has worn the piece as a Tiara was in 2017."

Translated from Norwegian, the "Vifte" Tiara, literally meaning "fan," aptly matches its name in design. This distinct piece, significantly smaller than conventional crowns, is crafted from gold, silver, and diamonds, and can be adorned either in the hair or as a necklace. It was long presumed to have been commissioned by Queen Victoria in 1887 as a gift for her granddaughter's coming of age.

Contrarily, a rare photograph depicts Queen Maud donning the tiara during the mourning period for Queen Victoria in 1901, leading some to speculate it was worn in remembrance of her grandmother. However, this interpretation has since been debunked.

Recent discussions on the Royal Jewels of the World Message Board have illuminated new insights regarding its provenance.

The tiara was actually bestowed upon Princess Maud for her wedding on July 22, 1896, a generous present from the Rothschild family.

The Vifte Tiara subsequently became part of Crown Prince Harald's inheritance and was shortly worn by Crown Princess Sonja.

In 2001, the tiara was gifted to Mette-Marit Tjessem on her marriage to Crown Prince Haakon of Norway, particularly styled as a necklace, which she also wore during a State Banquet in 2006. Remarkably, Crown Princess Mette-Marit has sported it as a tiara solely once, in 2017.

Переведи на формальный английский так, чтобы максимально лаконично звучало для носителей языка, но не сокращай намеренно текст. Где нужно измени и подправь. Чтобы было правильно для носителей "Королева Мод любила часто возвращаться на свою британскую родину, чтобы навестить родственников. Осенью 1938 года Мод вновь отправилась в Соединенное Королевство. У королевы был рак, и ей была оказана медицинская помощь. Однако после операции, проведенной в первые недели ноября, здоровье Мод ухудшилось, и она умерла от сердечной недостаточности. Позже выяснилось, что Мод взяла большую часть своих украшений с собой, намереваясь их почистить. 
Но драгоценности не были возвращены в Норвегию вместе с телом покойной королевы.
На самом деле это было одно из лучших событий, которые могли произойти, потому что два года спустя драгоценности все еще не были доставлены обратно в Норвегию, а это означало, что они были вне досягаемости, когда страна была оккупирована немецкими войсками. Вот почему король Haakon решил, что драгоценности Мод должны пока храниться в секретном месте в Великобритании.
Лишь в 1953 году драгоценности были возвращены сыном и невесткой Мод, наследным принцем Олавом и наследной принцессой Мартой, которые привезли их обратно в Норвегию после коронации королевы Елизаветы II. Но затем в 1954 году Марта неожиданно умерла, и драгоценности оставались в хранилищах долгие годы, пока норвежская королевская ювелирная коллекция не была разделена после свадьбы тогдашнего кронпринца Харальда в 1968 году."

Queen Maud frequently cherished returning to her British homeland to visit relatives. In the autumn of 1938, Maud once again traveled to the United Kingdom. The Queen was battling cancer and received medical treatment. However, following surgery in the early weeks of November, Maud’s health deteriorated, leading to her demise from heart failure. It was later revealed that Maud had taken a significant portion of her jewelry with her, intending to have it cleaned.

The jewels, however, were not returned to Norway with the Queen's body.

In hindsight, this became one of the more fortuitous outcomes, as two years later, the jewels had still not been returned to Norway, rendering them inaccessible during the country’s occupation by German forces. Consequently, King Haakon decided that Maud's jewels should remain in a secret location in Britain for the time being.

It was not until 1953 that the jewels were repatriated to Norway by Maud’s son and daughter-in-law, Crown Prince Olav and Crown Princess Märtha, who brought them back following the coronation of Queen Elizabeth II. However, in 1954, Märtha unexpectedly passed away, and the jewels remained in storage for many years until the Norwegian royal jewel collection was divided following the wedding of the then-Crown Prince Harald in 1968.

"Знаете ли вы, что норвежская королевская семья имеет британские королевские корни? Британская принцесса, вышедшая замуж за датского принца и ставшая первой королевой независимого Королевства Норвегия, королева Мод владела значительной коллекцией драгоценностей эдвардианской эпохи, большая часть которой до сих пор находится в норвежских королевских хранилищах. 
В этом видео мы поближе познакомимся с впечатляющими тиарами из коллекции королевы Мод, среди которых также есть важные ювелирные наследия британской королевской семьи.
"

Did you know that the Norwegian royal family has British royal ancestry? A British princess who married a Danish prince and became the first queen of an independent Kingdom of Norway, Queen Maud possessed a significant collection of Edwardian-era jewelry, much of which remains in the Norwegian royal vaults to this day.

In this video, we will delve into the impressive tiaras from Queen Maud's collection, which also include important jewels from the British royal family's heritage.
"Большую часть украшений, принадлежавших королеве Мод, ожидала тридцатилетняя спячка, вызванная судьбоносной войной и смертью королевы и наследной принцессы. "
Much of the jewelry owned by Queen Maud was subjected to a thirty-year dormancy, a period prompted by the cataclysmic war and the deaths of the Queen and the Crown Princess.

Свяжи логически между собой следующие абзацы: 
1: "Queen Maud of Wales was a notable figure in the history of the Norwegian monarchy. Born in London in 1869 to British King Edward VII and Alexandra of Denmark, her destiny intertwined with that of Norway when she became its first queen after the dissolution of the union with Sweden. Amid her reign, Queen Maud dazzled not only with her benevolence but also with her exquisite taste in attire, often bedecked in resplendent tiaras that adorned her with unmatched elegance."
2: "Much of the jewelry owned by Queen Maud was subjected to a thirty-year dormancy, a period prompted by the cataclysmic war and the deaths of the Queen and the Crown Princess."
3: "Queen Maud frequently cherished returning to her British homeland to visit relatives. In the autumn of 1938, Maud once again traveled to the United Kingdom. The Queen was battling cancer and received medical treatment. However, following surgery in the early weeks of November, Maud’s health deteriorated, leading to her demise from heart failure. It was later revealed that Maud had taken a significant portion of her jewelry with her, intending to have it cleaned.
The jewels, however, were not returned to Norway with the Queen’s body.
In hindsight, this became one of the more fortuitous outcomes, as two years later, the jewels had still not been returned to Norway, rendering them inaccessible during the country’s occupation by German forces. Consequently, King Haakon decided that Maud’s jewels should remain in a secret location in Britain for the time being.
It was not until 1953 that the jewels were repatriated to Norway by Maud’s son and daughter-in-law, Crown Prince Olav and Crown Princess Märtha, who brought them back following the coronation of Queen Elizabeth II. However, in 1954, Märtha unexpectedly passed away, and the jewels remained in storage for many years until the Norwegian royal jewel collection was divided following the wedding of the then-Crown Prince Harald in 1968.
"

Queen Maud of Wales emerged as a prominent figure within the Norwegian monarchy. Born in London in 1869 to British King Edward VII and Alexandra of Denmark, her life took a significant turn when she became Norway's first queen following the dissolution of the union with Sweden. Her reign was marked not only by her benevolent influence but also by her exquisite sartorial choices, particularly her selection of splendid tiaras that accentuated her elegance uniquely.

The historical backdrop to Queen Maud's extensive jewelry collection involves a lengthy period of inactivity that spanned thirty years. This hiatus was mainly due to the tumultuous period of the war and the subsequent deaths of both the Queen and the Crown Princess. Such events underscore the intricate relationship between personal tragedy and broader historical events in the life of the royal family.

Queen Maud's fondness for her British homeland led her back to the United Kingdom in the autumn of 1938, as she sought treatment for cancer. Despite undergoing surgery, her health swiftly declined, leading to her death from heart failure. Unknown to many at the time, Maud had transported a significant part of her jewelry collection to Britain, presumably for cleaning. This decision inadvertently shielded the treasure from being seized during Norway's occupation by German forces, as the jewels were not returned with Maud's body to Norway.

It was only in 1953 that these precious items were repatriated to Norway, thanks to the efforts of Maud’s son, Crown Prince Olav, and his wife, Crown Princess Märtha. Their return coincided with the coronation of Queen Elizabeth II. Yet, following Märtha's unexpected death in 1954, the collection was kept in storage for an extended period. Ultimately, the Norwegian royal jewel collection, including Queen Maud's pieces, underwent a division that corresponded with Crown Prince Harald's wedding in 1968, marking a significant moment in the country's royal heritage and the enduring legacy of Queen Maud's elegance and taste.

Чтобы было правильно для носителей "Большую часть украшений, принадлежавших королеве Мод, ждала непростая судьба. Прошло несколько десятков лет прежде чем эти украшения вновь появились на публике. И вот что произошло."
The majority of jewelry once owned by Queen Maud faced a complex destiny. Several decades passed before these pieces reemerged in public. Here is what transpired.

"Сейчас я расскажу вам небольшую предысторию, которая понадобится нам, когда мы будем разбираться кому и какие драгоценности впоследствии достались.
"
I will now provide a brief background that will be necessary as we explore to whom and which specific jewels were eventually bequeathed.
"Сейчас я расскажу вам небольшую предысторию, которая понадобится нам, когда мы будем разбираться, как были поделены драгоценности королевы"
I will now share a brief background, which will be essential as we delve into how the Queen's jewels were divided.

Сделай рерайт в формальном стиле,  чтобы максимально лаконично звучало для носителей английского языка. Где нужно измени и подправь. Чтобы было правильно для носителей "In 1902, it was reported in the British Press (h/t to Knightship) that King Edward VII gave a Turquoise Tiara as a Gift to Queen Alexandra for her 58th Birthday.

While Queen Alexandra was mentioned wearing Turquoise Jewels on various occasions, there does not seem to be any public photographs of her wearing the piece.

After Queen Alexandra’s death in 1925, the Turquoise Tiara was inherited by her younger daughter, Queen Maud of Norway, who was notably pictured wearing the Turquoise Tiara for the Wedding Gala of her only son, Crown Prince Olav of Norway, and Princess Martha of Sweden in 1929. The Tiara paired particularly well with Queen Maud’s extensive collection of Turquoise Jewels, many of which were wedding presents.

when the jewels were split between the King and his two sisters, and it was Princess Astrid who got Queen Alexandra’s Turquoise Circlet.

Princess Astrid began wearing Queen Alexandra’s Turquoise Tiara soon after, often pairing it with pieces from Queen Maud’s collection of Turquoise jewels.

the turquoises have been a key part of Princess Astrid’s gala jewelry. She wore them later for one of the most important Norwegian royal events in a generation: the wedding of Crown Prince Haakon and Crown Princess Mette-Marit in Oslo in August 2001. Also in February 2017, she wore them for official portraits to mark her own 85th birthday. 
Princess Astrid has stated that the Tiara and Turquoise Parure will eventually return to the main line of the Royal Family, but there is no doubt we will continue to see this Tiara for years to come!"

In 1902, reports emerged in the British press (courtesy of Knightship) highlighting King Edward VII's presentation of a Turquoise Tiara to Queen Alexandra on her 58th birthday. 

Though Queen Alexandra was observed on several occasions adorned in turquoise jewels, no public images of her wearing this particular tiara have surfaced.

Following Queen Alexandra's passing in 1925, the tiara was bequeathed to her younger daughter, Queen Maud of Norway. Queen Maud was notably depicted wearing the Turquoise Tiara at the wedding gala of her son, Crown Prince Olav of Norway, and Princess Martha of Sweden in 1929. This tiara complemented Queen Maud's extensive turquoise jewelry collection, much of which she received as wedding gifts.

Subsequently, the jewels were distributed between the King and his sisters, with Princess Astrid receiving Queen Alexandra's Turquoise Circlet.

Princess Astrid began to showcase the Turquoise Tiara shortly thereafter, frequently coordinating it with items from Queen Maud’s turquoise collection.

These turquoise pieces have become staples in Princess Astrid's gala jewelry collection. Notably, she donned them during Crown Prince Haakon and Crown Princess Mette-Marit's wedding in Oslo in August 2001. In February 2017, she wore them again for official portraits commemorating her 85th birthday.

Princess Astrid has indicated that the Tiara and the Turquoise Parure will eventually be reintegrated into the primary lineage of the Royal Family, ensuring the continued visibility of this tiara in the years ahead.
Сделай рерайт на формальном английском "Princess Astrid has indicated that that the tiara will return to the Norwegian royal vaults one day after her passing — hopefully far in the future."
Princess Astrid has expressed that the tiara will be returned to the Norwegian royal vaults following her demise, which is hoped to be in the distant future.

Перепиши текст, заменив все цифры, римские цифры и аббревиатуры буквами так, как они должны читаться "Hello, everyone! Welcome back to my channel.
Did you know that the Norwegian royal family has British royal ancestry? A British princess who married a Danish prince and became the first queen of an independent Kingdom of Norway, Queen Maud, possessed a significant collection of Edwardian-era jewelry, much of which remains in the Norwegian royal vaults to this day.
In this video, we will delve into the impressive tiaras from Queen Maud’s collection, which also include important jewels from the British royal family’s heritage.
Before we begin, please support my channel by clicking the like and subscribe buttons. Thank you.
Queen Maud of Wales was a notable figure in the history of the Norwegian monarchy. Born in London in 1869 to British King Edward VII and Alexandra of Denmark, her destiny intertwined with that of Norway when she became its first queen after the dissolution of the union with Sweden. Amid her reign, Queen Maud dazzled not only with her benevolence but also with her exquisite taste in attire, often bedecked in resplendent tiaras that adorned her with unmatched elegance.
The majority of jewelry once owned by Queen Maud faced a complex destiny. Several decades passed before these pieces reemerged in public. I will now share a brief background, which will be essential as we delve into how the Queen’s jewels were divided.
Queen Maud frequently cherished returning to her British homeland to visit relatives. In the autumn of 1938, Maud once again traveled to the United Kingdom. The Queen was battling cancer and received medical treatment. However, following surgery in the early weeks of November, Maud’s health deteriorated, leading to her demise from heart failure. It was later revealed that Maud had taken a significant portion of her jewelry with her, intending to have it cleaned. 
The jewels, however, were not returned to Norway with the Queen’s body.
In hindsight, this became one of the more fortuitous outcomes, as two years later, the jewels had still not been returned to Norway, rendering them inaccessible during the country’s occupation by German forces. Consequently, King Haakon decided that Maud’s jewels should remain in a secret location in Britain for the time being.
It was not until 1953 that the jewels were repatriated to Norway by Maud’s son and daughter-in-law, Crown Prince Olav and Crown Princess Märtha, who brought them back following the coronation of Queen Elizabeth II. However, in 1954, Märtha unexpectedly passed away, and the jewels remained in storage for many years until the Norwegian royal jewel collection was divided following the wedding of the then-Crown Prince Harald in 1968.
Queen Maud’s Pearl Tiara
Queen Maud's Pearl and Diamond Tiara, a wedding gift from her parents, King Edward VII and Queen Alexandra, for her marriage to Prince Carl of Denmark in 1896. Featuring a diamond base adorned with pearls, it became a staple of Queen Maud's attire after she became Queen of Norway in 1905.
The front part of the tiara can be removed, and the rest can be worn in a lower version.
In 1968, the tiara passed to Queen Sonja upon her marriage to Crown Prince Harald. However, in 1995, it was stolen in an armed robbery at Garrard in London, where it was being repaired. Despite efforts to recover it, the original tiara was never found.

To compensate for the loss, Garrard crafted a replica for Queen Sonja, although it retains the name "Queen Maud's Tiara." This tiara is cherished within the royal family and has two versions: the original full version and a smaller one created by rearranging the pearls, worn by Princess Märtha Louise and Crown Princess Mette-Marit on special occasions.
The Maltese Circlet
Featuring Diamond Fleur-de-Lys and Maltese Crosses on top of a Diamond Base, the Diamond Circlet was made by Garrard for Queen Alexandra in 1910. Originally intended as a crown for Queen Alexandra to wear during her widowhood, it likely served as a replacement for the George IV State Diadem. Notably, it was designed to house the renowned Cullinan VI Diamond.
Upon Queen Alexandra's passing in 1925, the Diamond Circlet, without the Cullinan VI, passed into the possession of her daughter, Queen Maud of Norway.
Queen Maud made notable alterations to the tiara, removing the fleur-de-lys to differentiate it from its British counterpart. She frequently adorned herself with the tiara, even bringing it to the UK for King George VI's coronation. However, tragically, upon her unexpected demise in 1938, the tiara remained in Britain and was not returned to Norway until 1953.
Today, Queen Sonja, Queen Maud's granddaughter-in-law, proudly wears the tiara. Often, she opts to wear it without the crosses, though occasionally, she adds them.
Queen Maud’s Diamond Tiara
This diamond tiara, much like Maud's Pearl and Diamond Tiara, was a wedding gift when she married Prince Carl of Denmark in 1896 (who later became King Haakon VII of Norway). A sizable piece, it features an intricate design with two rows of diamonds on a collet base and diamond uprights on top. Presented to the bride by "Lady and Gentlemen Friends."
Eventually, the tiara came into the possession of Princess Ragnhild, Maud's granddaughter and the oldest child of King Olav V and Crown Princess Märtha. 
Ragnhild, who married Erling Lorentzen in 1953 and spent much of her life in Brazil, lived a relatively private life and attended royal events infrequently. 
After her death in 2012, Queen Maud’s Diamond Tiara is one of the family heirlooms that is said to pass back into the collection of the main Royal Family, though it has remained unworn. 
Norwegian royal watchers are waiting for this grand tiara to reappear. But when will it happen?
Queen Maud’s Vifte Tiara
Translated from Norwegian, the “Vifte” Tiara, literally meaning “fan,” aptly matches its name in design. This distinct piece, significantly smaller than conventional crowns, is crafted from gold, silver, and diamonds, and can be adorned either in the hair or as a necklace. It was long presumed to have been commissioned by Queen Victoria in 1887 as a gift for her granddaughter’s coming of age.
A rare photograph depicts Queen Maud donning the tiara during the mourning period for Queen Victoria in 1901, leading some to speculate it was worn in remembrance of her grandmother. However, this interpretation has since been debunked.
Recent discussions on the Royal Jewels of the World Message Board have illuminated new insights regarding its provenance.
The tiara was actually bestowed upon Princess Maud for her wedding on July 22, 1896, a generous present from the Rothschild family.
The Vifte Tiara subsequently became part of Crown Prince Harald’s inheritance and was shortly worn by Crown Princess Sonja.
In 2001, the tiara was gifted to Mette-Marit Tjessem on her marriage to Crown Prince Haakon of Norway, with the piece worn as a necklace, which she also wore during a State Banquet in 2006. Remarkably, Crown Princess Mette-Marit has sported it as a tiara solely once, in 2017.
The Turquoise Circlet
In 1902, reports emerged in the British press highlighting King Edward VII’s presentation of a Turquoise Tiara to Queen Alexandra on her 58th birthday.
Though Queen Alexandra was observed on several occasions adorned in turquoise jewels, no public images of her wearing this particular tiara have surfaced.
Following Queen Alexandra’s passing in 1925, the tiara was bequeathed to her younger daughter, Queen Maud of Norway. Queen Maud was notably depicted wearing the Turquoise Tiara at the wedding gala of her son, Crown Prince Olav of Norway, and Princess Martha of Sweden in 1929. This tiara complemented Queen Maud’s extensive turquoise jewelry collection, much of which she received as wedding gifts.
Subsequently, the jewels were distributed between the King and his sisters, with Princess Astrid receiving Queen Alexandra’s Turquoise Circlet.
Princess Astrid began to showcase the Turquoise Tiara shortly thereafter, frequently coordinating it with items from Queen Maud’s turquoise collection.
These turquoise pieces have become staples in Princess Astrid’s gala jewelry collection. Notably, she donned them during Crown Prince Haakon and Crown Princess Mette-Marit’s wedding in Oslo in August 2001. In February 2017, she wore them again for official portraits commemorating her 85th birthday.
Princess Astrid has stated that the tiara will be returned to the Norwegian royal vaults upon her passing, which I hope will be in the distant future.

Thank you for watching this video. Share your impressions in the comments and support my channel by subscribing and liking. Thank you.

"

Originally intended as a crown for Queen Alexandra to wear during her widowhood, it likely served as a replacement for the George the Fourth State Diadem. Notably, it was designed to house the renowned Cullinan the Sixth Diamond. Upon Queen Alexandra's passing in nineteen twenty-five, the Diamond Circlet, without the Cullinan the Sixth, passed into the possession of her daughter, Queen Maud of Norway. Queen Maud’s Vifte Tiara Translated from Norwegian, the “Vifte” Tiara, literally meaning “fan,” aptly matches its name in design. This distinct piece, significantly smaller than conventional crowns, is crafted from gold, silver, and diamonds, and can be adorned either in the hair or as a necklace. It was long presumed to have been commissioned by Queen Victoria in eighteen eighty-seven as a gift for her granddaughter’s coming of age. A rare photograph depicts Queen Maud donning the tiara during the mourning period for Queen Victoria in nineteen o one, leading some to speculate it was worn in remembrance of her grandmother. However, this interpretation has since been debunked. Recent discussions on the Royal Jewels of the World Message Board have illuminated new insights regarding its provenance. The tiara was actually bestowed upon Princess Maud for her wedding on July twenty-second, eighteen ninety-six, a generous present from the Rothschild family. The Vifte Tiara subsequently became part of Crown Prince Harald’s inheritance and was shortly worn by Crown Princess Sonja. In two thousand and one, the tiara was gifted to Mette-Marit Tjessem on her marriage to Crown Prince Haakon of Norway, with the piece worn as a necklace, which she also wore during a State Banquet in two thousand and six. Remarkably, Crown Princess Mette-Marit has sported it as a tiara solely once, in two thousand and seventeen. The Turquoise Circlet In nineteen o two, reports emerged in the British press highlighting King Edward the Seventh’s presentation of a Turquoise Tiara to Queen Alexandra on her fifty-eighth birthday. Though Queen Alexandra was observed on several occasions adorned in turquoise jewels, no public images of her wearing this particular tiara have surfaced. Following Queen Alexandra’s passing in nineteen twenty-five, the tiara was bequeathed to her younger daughter, Queen Maud of Norway. Queen Maud was notably depicted wearing the Turquoise Tiara at the wedding gala of her son, Crown Prince Olav of Norway, and Princess Martha of Sweden in nineteen twenty-nine. This tiara complemented Queen Maud’s extensive turquoise jewelry collection, much of which she received as wedding gifts. Subsequently, the jewels were distributed between the King and his sisters, with Princess Astrid receiving Queen Alexandra’s Turquoise Circlet. Princess Astrid began to showcase the Turquoise Tiara shortly thereafter, frequently coordinating it with items from Queen Maud’s turquoise collection. These turquoise pieces have become staples in Princess Astrid’s gala jewelry collection. Notably, she donned them during Crown Prince Haakon and Crown Princess Mette-Marit’s wedding in Oslo in August two thousand and one. In February two thousand and seventeen, she wore them again for official portraits commemorating her eighty-fifth birthday. Princess Astrid has stated that the tiara will be returned to the Norwegian royal vaults upon her passing, which I hope will be in the distant future. Thank you for watching this video.
